records.ety: [OE. fleten, fleoten, to swim, AS. fletan to swim, float; akin to D. vlieten to flow, OS. fliotan, OHG. fliozzan, G. fliessen, Icel. fljōta to float, flow, Sw. flyta, D. flyde, L. pluere to rain, Gr. plei^n to sail, swim, float, Skr. plu to swim, sail. √84. Cf. Fleet, n. & a., Float, Pluvial, Flow.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. fleeted; p. pr. & vb. n. fleeting.]

quotes: And in frail wood on Adrian Gulf doth fleet.

defs: To fly swiftly; to pass over quickly; to hasten; to flit as a light substance.
quotes: All the unaccomplished works of Nature's hand, . . . Dissolved on earth, fleet hither.

defs: To slip on the whelps or the barrel of a capstan or windlass; -- said of a cable or hawser.
To move or change in position; -- said of persons; as, the crew fleeted aft.
To pass over rapidly; to skin the surface of; as, a ship that fleets the gulf.
To hasten over; to cause to pass away lightly, or in mirth and joy.
quotes: Many young gentlemen flock to him, and fleet the time carelessly.

defs: To draw apart the blocks of; -- said of a tackle.
To cause to slip down the barrel of a capstan or windlass, as a rope or chain.
To move or change in position; used only in special phrases; as, of fleet aft the crew.
quotes: We got the long “stick” . . . down and “fleeted” aft, where it was secured.

records.ety: [Cf. Icel. fljōtr quick. See Fleet, v. i.]

defs: Swift in motion; moving with velocity; light and quick in going from place to place; nimble.
quotes: In mail their horses clad, yet fleet and strong.

defs: Light; superficially thin; not penetrating deep, as soil.

records.ety: [OE. flete, fleote, AS. flet ship, fr. fletan to float, swim. See Fleet, v. i. and cf. Float.]

defs: A number of vessels in company, especially war vessels; also, the collective naval force of a country, etc.

records.ety: [AS. flet a place where vessels float, bay, river; akin to D. vliet rill, brook, G. fliess. See Fleet, v. i.]

defs: A flood; a creek or inlet; a bay or estuary; a river; -- obsolete, except as a place name, -- as Fleet Street in London.
quotes: Together wove we nets to entrap the fish In floods and sedgy fleets.

defs: A former prison in London, which originally stood near a stream, the Fleet (now filled up).

records.ety: [AS. flēt cream, fr. fletan to float. See Fleet, v. i.]

defs: To take the cream from; to skim.
